one_line_takeaway: A position paper arguing that AI (LLM) safety/security audits which examine foundation models or deployed applications in isolation miss cumulative 'cascade' effects from interacting supply-chain components (guardrails, quantization, prompts, personalization, tool use) and proposing this cascade as a new object of auditing analysis and research agenda -- with no original empirical evaluation.
key_finding: There is no empirical finding -- the paper's central claim is an argumentative/conceptual one: existing LLM audit practice bifurcates into (a) FM capability/component audits that examine components in isolation, and (b) application-level audits that treat the deployed system as a black box, and neither captures how effects compound across the supply chain (the cascade). The paper concludes that this gap should motivate a 'paradigm shift' toward systems-oriented, cascade-aware audits that combine technical methods (e.g. lineage/decision-provenance tracking, cross-configuration evaluation) with human-centered/interdisciplinary methods (stakeholder-focused studies, participatory audit frameworks).
core_contribution: This is a conceptual/position paper (not an empirical study) that introduces 'the cascade' as an analytical concept for AI safety/security auditing. The cascade captures how socio-technical components along an AI (specifically LLM) supply chain -- e.g. base model, quantization, safety guardrails, system prompts, RAG/knowledge bases, tool use, memory, personalization -- interact, compound, amplify, dampen, or nullify each other's effects, producing cumulative downstream consequences that neither model-centric nor whole-application audits capture. The paper's own contributions, as stated in its 'Statement of Contribution' section, are: (i) identifying gaps in current AI auditing approaches (using LLMs as the case study) via a literature survey of FM capability/component audits and application-level audits; (ii) characterizing cascades through two modalities -- the 'component cascade' (how transformation effects compound across components) and the 'stakeholder cascade' (how distributed visibility/control across developers, deployers, and users obscures accountability); and (iii) proposing research directions (technical, e.g. lineage tracking and cross-component behavioral tracing; and human-centered/interdisciplinary, e.g. stakeholder-focused studies and participatory audit frameworks) for treating the cascade itself as an object of audit analysis.
threat_model: The paper does not define a formal adversarial threat model with an attacker, capabilities, or goals. Instead it frames a systemic auditing/visibility gap: each stakeholder (model provider, application developer, end-user) can typically only observe and control the components they directly manage, not the full supply chain, so harmful or unsafe behaviors can emerge from component interactions (e.g. conflicting safety modifications, quantization interacting with guardrails, personalization overriding safety constraints) that are invisible to any single auditor. The one adversarial angle mentioned in passing is a 'Balanced Transparency' tension: revealing safety-related system prompts or other component details to improve auditability could itself enable tailor-made jailbreak attacks -- but this is raised as a tension/research consideration, not developed into an attack methodology.
limitations: Explicitly acknowledged/implicit limitations found in the text: the paper itself states that the two modalities (component and stakeholder cascade) 'are not exhaustive viewpoints' and are illustrative rather than complete. No original empirical validation, benchmark, dataset, or system implementation is provided -- the cascade concept and its associated taxonomies (five transformation types, five technical implications) are proposed but not tested against real deployed systems or measured with any metric. The two illustrative examples (chatbot, mental-health support scenario) are hypothetical narratives, not case studies of actual audited systems. The paper's own footnote clarifies its use of 'cascade' is distinct from technical ML/software-engineering cascade models (e.g. cascading classifiers, waterfall models), which could cause terminology confusion with existing literature. The 'Balanced Transparency' tension is noted but not resolved: the paper flags that increasing component-level auditability could itself enable jailbreak attacks, without proposing a concrete mitigation.
